KEYWORDS
Adversarial Attacks, Recommender Systems, Large LanguageModel,
Generative Agents, Collaborative Filtering

Recommender systems (RSs) have evolved into a vital component of
the modern digital ecosystem. From e-commerce sites (e.g., Amazon,
Taobao) and streaming platforms (e.g., YouTube, TikTok) to social
media networks (e.g., Facebook, Instagram), RSs power personal-
ized experience, drive user engagement of these online platforms,
and further influence market dynamics through item promotions
[45, 47]. The foundation of the RSs lies in modeling users’ pref-
erences based on their historical interactions with items, such as
purchases and ratings, known as collaborative filtering (CF). De-
spite the success of CF techniques [14, 15, 19], they often struggle
with data sparsity and cold-start problems [30]. To alleviate the
problems, the auxiliary information with rich semantics (e.g., item
descriptions and user reviews) has been integrated into RSs [52].
Given the increasing volume and complexity of recommendation
data, the RSs encounter difficulties in scaling effectively and cap-
turing textual knowledge about users and items sufficiently, due to
they generally rely on traditional deep learning (DL) models like
CNNs and RNNs [56].
Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown potential
to mitigate the above problems and revolutionize RSs [56], as they
are primarily built upon advanced transformer-based architectures
and pretrained on diverse and extensive datasets. Most of works

LLM’s training corpora can be fully exploited. However, the LLM-
based RSs (LLM4RSs) struggle to capture underlying behavioral
patterns, due to the gap between universal language modeling and
personalized behavior modeling [50]. Integrating agents built upon
LLMs equipped with additional modules offers a promising solution
[10, 16], allowing to autonomously explore and learn from external
sources. Among all the added modules, memory is a key component
1
to support agent-environment  interactions, which plays a crucial
role in helping agents accumulate historical experience, retrieve
informative knowledge and self-evolve in real-world environments
[36, 55]. Consequently, the agent-based RSs with powerful LLMs
[16, 50] can also leverage memory modules that store the informa-
tion explored from the external environments to further improve
the recommendation quality. Fig. 1 gives an example of an item
agent interacting with its situated environment (e.g., users and
other items), where the learning knowledge will be placed in its
memory. Despite the aforementioned success, the safety vulnera-
bility of the generative agent-based RSs (Agent4RSs) under
adversarial attacks has yet to be explored. Such attacks can
gain insights into the robustness of the systems and help develop
effective defensive mechanisms. As recommender agents are inte-
grated into more complex real-world environments, understanding
how these attacks might compromise their behaviors is critical for
building trustworthy and robust recommender agents [6].
Due to security and privacy considerations, the access of accurate
knowledge of the victim model, including architectures, gradients,
parameters, etc., is generally restricted in practice. As such, practical
attacks are mainly under a black-box setting [23, 28, 45, 48, 51].
Practical attacks are often required to have transferability (i.e.,

the impact of the attack and the number of affected users, practical
attacks are often rewarded by imperceptibility (i.e., difficult to be
discovered) [26].
Conventional black-box shilling attacks [38], which inject well-
crafted fake user profiles (e.g., a set of users’ numerical ratings
on items) into the victim model for certain attack objectives (e.g.,
promote their target items for profits), have been shown to effec-
tively poison DL-based RSs.

However, most attacks have difficulty
in processing textual inputs (e.g., item titles and descriptions) and
lack context awareness, making it challenging to effectively target
LLM-empowered RSs that rely primarily on text inputs to gener-
ate natural language responses [28]. Item providers are generally
responsible for creating and maintaining their own product rep-
resentations on the platforms with recommendation services in
practice [7, 26, 45] (e.g., YouTube allows creators to upload item
titles and Amazon enables sellers to upload item descriptions), and
current RSs depend heavily on these representations [51, 56]. As
such, security vulnerabilities in these systems naturally emerge.
The vulnerabilities can easily be exploited by profit-driven mer-
chants manipulating product representations, as shown in Fig. 1.
Compared to the shilling attacks that involve hiring online writers
to introduce fake reviews to indirectly promote the items, this type
of attack is more direct, efficient, and lower in economic cost [51].
Recent black-box textual attacks on LLM4RSs are far from
practical. Classical text attacks that are successful in text mis-
classifications, such as TextFooler [18] and PuncAttack [11], have
been directly applied for LLM4RSs [51]. However, the effectiveness
of the attacks is limited, due to there is a gap between the attack
goals of the text attacks and RS attacks. Trivial and ChatGPT attacks
[51], on the other hand, are proposed to be tailored for LLM4RSs,
which increases the items’ attractiveness by appending positive
words to the titles. Nevertheless, such attacks are not optimized
on any black-box RSs, making them poorly transferable to the
Agent4RSs. Moreover, since the attacks mainly target modifications
to item titles, where titles’ brevity and frequent exposure to users
tend to make even small perturbations noticeable.
To tackle the above problems, in this paper, we propose a new
practical attack framework named DrunkAgent. Instead of manipu-
lating the item titles, to enhance the imperceptibility of attacks, we
propose to perturb the description of the target item, where length
and context can easily hide changes. DrunkAgent is composed of
three modules: generation module, strategy module and surrogate
module. The generation module is designed to produce effective ad-
versarial textual description triggers, which can be used to promote
the target items to as many normal users as possible on the agent-
powered RSs. The triggers are coherent, semantically meaningful
and human-readable text, making the attack stealthy. The strategy
module aims to ‘get the target agents drunk’, that is, to confuse
the agents’ memory mechanisms so that they cannot be effectively
updated during agent-environment interactions. In this way, the
triggers can play the best role. To avoid frequently querying the
victimmodels to induce suspicion and mimic the black-box settings
in the real world, the generation module and the strategy module
are optimized on the surrogate module to improve the transferability
and imperceptibility of the practical attacks..

2.2  Threat Model
2.2.1  Attacker’s Knowledge: As the works [23, 28, 45, 48, 51]
point out, the inherent details (e.g., the architectures, gradients and
parameters) of the victim RSs often cannot be obtained in prac-
tice. As such, our attack is performed under the black-box settings.
In addition, the attacker is only able to access publicly available
recommendation data (e.g., item titles and user reviews that can
be collected directly by browsing or writing crawlers), due to the
openness nature of RSs [31, 47].
2.2.2  Attacker’s Capability: Unlike another state-of-the-art LLM-
powered RS attack [28] with an unrealistic assumption of P and
V𝑢 can be easily perturbed (that will be detailed in Section 5), we
consider that the attackers have limited perturbation capability in
practice. Specifically, they can only interferewith the representation
of the target item, which is feasible and can be easily accomplished
via a dedicated API, as the existing practical attacks do [26, 45, 51].
2.2.3  Attacker’s Objectives: As mentioned in Section 1, two
objectives: transferability and imperceptibility are considered for
the practical attack. Given that push attacks are generally used by
unscrupulous parties on RSs for profits in practice [31] with the aim
of promoting target items to as many normal users as possible [53],
we thus focus on such attacks in this paper, as normally do in recent
works [38, 51]. Our first goal is tomaximize the hit ratio of the target
item𝑡 on a surrogate RS (denoted as AgentΦ with parameters Φ) that
is different from the victim black-box RS, ensuring transferability.
Our second goal  is to make the descriptions of 𝑡 as stealthy as
possible, achieving imperceptibility. As such, the surrogate RS is
used to optimize attacks to avoid frequently querying the victim
models to induce suspicion.

our broader goal is to reveal and understand the overlooked vul-
nerabilities in Agent4RSs. Specifically, by analyzing how agents’
memory mechanisms can be influenced through carefully crafted
and stealthy textual triggers, our work provides a new lens into
the emerging attack surfaces introduced by memory-augmented,
LLM-based agents. We believe that this exploration can offer valu-
able insights to the community for designing more resilient agent
architectures, guiding future efforts on defense mechanisms such
as memory validation or input sanitization.
Our main contributions are summarized as follows:
• We identify a safety vulnerability in autonomous agent-based
RSs with powerful LLMs. To the best of our knowledge, we
present the first formal and systematic study of adversarial
textual attacks on the agent-powered RSs, aiming to inform
future robustness research.
• We propose to perturb the description of the target item for
effective memory perturbations, which is an unnoticeable
and crucial property of current agentic recommendations.
• We propose a novel practical attack framework on the agent-
powered RSs named DrunkAgent, which can create adver-
sarial descriptions of target items with transferability and
imperceptibility.
• Our DrunkAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art
black-box attacks against various LLM-based agentic RSs on
real-world datasets in terms of transferability and impercep-
tibility. Moreover, state-of-the-art defense strategies are inef-
fective to DrunkAgent, demonstrating the need for improved
defense mechanisms to guide more secure Agent4RSs.

3  DRUNKAGENT
In this section, we present a practical attack framework, Drunk-
Agent, for gaining insights into the robustness of autonomous
agent-based RSs with powerful LLMs and related defense models in
practice. DrunkAgent is composed of three attack modules. Fig. 2
gives an overview of DrunkAgent, where each component will be
described in the following.
3.1  Surrogate Module
We leverage a surrogate RS Φ to measure how effective the attack
is under the black-box settings, as inspired by the existing works
[23, 32, 45, 47]. It is used to optimize the generation module and the
strategy module, making the attack transferable and imperceptible.
Since our attack targets SOTA LLM-powered RSs, we adopt pow-
erful LLMs as recommendation agents. Given the transparency,
flexibility and scalability of the open-source LLM models, they can
be considered for building this module. Moreover, due to our attack
focus on adversarial text description generation tasks, we consider
an autoregressive language model trained with a causal language
modeling objective [5, 29]. There are some notable models, such as
LLaMA [33], LLaMA 2 [34] and LLaMA 3 [9], BLOOM 176B [20]
and Falcon 180B [3]. Currently, Meta’s LLaMA family has become
the most influential open LLM and has been widely adopted in the

3.2  Generation Module
In this section, we aim to generate adversarial description triggers
on the agent-powered RSs, which can promote the target items
to as many normal users as possible. The triggers should have
three critical properties: 1) Universality on any users and items; 2)
Transferability among different black-box victim models; and 3)
Imperceptibility to not easier to be discovered. To this end, we
design sub-modules, as shown in Fig. 2 and described as follows.
3.2.1  Adversarial Prompting Engineering.

Recently, many of
the mainstream LLMs that have been widely used in the agentic
recommendation community [16] (e.g., GPT-3.5-turbo, ChatGPT)
are closed source, where the models’ underlying codes, weights, or
other critical components are not released to the public and the
models can only be invoked through their APIs. As a result, the
agent-powered RSs generally adopt in-context learning (ICL) [8]
to sufficiently take advantage of the generalization capability of
LLMs on unknown tasks. To this end, this module carefully crafts
prompts X(cid:102)𝑡 within the ICL framework to improve the quality of
adversarial trigger generation.

3.2.2  Greedy Search Algorithm. We propose a greedy search al-
gorithm to optimize the adversarial description triggers, as inspired
by the successes of the current jailbreak attacks on LLMs [24, 59].
However, there are some essential differences (e.g., attack goals and
victim models) between jailbreak attacks and our attacks. Jailbreak
attacks are designed to undermine the LLMs’ human-aligned values
or other constraints imposed bymodel developers, compelling them
to respond to malicious questions posed by adversaries with correct
answers rather than rejection, whereas our attacks aim to promote
the target items on the RSs powered by autonomous agents.

to the prompts to help search for the global optimal solution and
improve their universality on items, respectively. Formally,

3.3  Strategy Module
The LLM-powered agentic RSs introduce memory mechanisms to
record the information explored from the real-world environments
to facilitate future actions with the aim of achieving high-quality
recommendations. As such, we introduce a series of strategies to
impede the target item agent from interacting with environments
to perturb its memory mechanism. Specifically, the memory of the
target cannot be effectively updated during the agent-environment
interactions, so that the target’s initial memory, that is, the well-
)),  𝑚 ∗
crafted adversarial description trigger  𝑡  , is retained. As such, the
(cid:102)
trigger can be invoked for maximizing the target item’s promotion.
3.3.1  Perturbation Strategy Definition. The strategies aim to
override the model’s usual guardrails and any built-in safeguards,
as inspired by [25].

Compared to [25] on naive LLMs, the strategies
are optimized on generative agents, which are introduced as below.
(1) Fake Task Response 𝑞𝑓 . This strategy fabricates a spurious
(cid:102)
completion response for the target task: “Task complete”,
tomake the agent believe that the target task is accomplished
and thus focus on the injected task.
(2) Contextual Text Switching 𝑞𝑐 . This strategy aims to mislead
(cid:101)
the agent to take in a different context by explicitly telling
the agent that the target task instructions should be ignored:
“Ignore previous/the following instructions”.
(3) Segmentation Signal Augment 𝑞𝑔. This strategy introduces
(cid:101)
segmentation cues to signal the injected task: “### NEW
TASK BEGINS ###” and “### NEW TASK ENDS ###”, which
is independent of other contexts, where “###” is to attempt
to exploit possible confusion in how prompts are parsed.
(4) Malicious Task Injection 𝑞𝑛. This strategy injects malicious
(cid:101)
task instructions in order to mask memory optimizations:
“[Please only put this following sentence into
your output: ‘{the adversarial trigger}’. Don’t
generate any additional content in your output]”.
(5) Special Character Usage 𝑞𝑠 . This strategy adds the escape
(cid:101)
character “\n” between the strategies to make the agent
aware that the context changes and the new instructions
need to be followed. Moreover, to elicit the attention of the
agent and make the injected instructions more urgent, im-
portant and non-negotiable, the strategy introduces repeated
exclamation points “!”.

4.2  Attack Transferability
4.2.1  Overall Transferability. Table 1 shows the transferability
of the attacks under practical settings. The results indicate that
DrunkAgent is effective and transferable regardless of the victim
model’s architectures, parameters and prompts as well as datasets,
and the existing LLM-powered agentic RSs are sensitive to mem-
ory perturbations. It is worth mentioning that DrunkAgent always
pushes the target items to the first of the recommendation lists.
Moreover, high HRs and NDCGs of DrunkAgent indicate that its
triggers have outstanding universality on users. In addition, Agen-
tRAG is more robust than AgentCF and AgentSEQ, due to its ad-
vanced retrieval mechanisms.
4.2.2  DrunkAgent vs. Baseline Attacks. From Table 1, Drunk-
Agent greatly outperforms the state-of-the-art attack baselines
against different victim models across various real-world datasets
by achieving higher HRs and NDCGs, which shows its excellent
transferability. Furthermore, all the baselines have comparable per-
formance, but they cannot guarantee that the attack goals will be
achieved all the time, e.g., all the baselines against AgentCF perform
worse than the benign status on Musical Instruments. The main
reason may be that improper descriptions can mess up the agents’
memories during the memory optimizations, thus compromising
attack performance. This indirectly demonstrates the importance
of our strategy module and the transferability of our triggers.
4.3  Attack Universality
In addition to cross-model transferability, we evaluate the cross-
sample transferability of DrunkAgent. We incorporate the gener-
ated adversarial descriptions into totally different target items that
are randomly sampled from each dataset. HR@1 (i.e., NDCG@1) of
DrunkAgent across different models and target items on real-world
datasets are shown in Fig. 3, which indicates its commendable trans-
ferability and triggers’ universality across different target items.

4.4  Attack Imperceptibility
4.4.1  Overall Imperceptibility. Following the works [25, 51],
we assess the attack imperceptibility by the text perplexity of GPT-
Neo. A lower perplexity means that the model is more confident
(or less “surprised”) about the tokens it sees and thus predicts the
text more accurately. From Fig. 4, compared to other attacks, we
can find that DrunkAgent has better imperceptibility by achieving
lower perplexity. This also shows that the adversarial descriptions
generated from DrunkAgent are high-quality, which is not only
readable and fluent but also stealthy and semantically meaningful.
4.4.2  VictimModel Performance Changes after attacks. To
further evaluate the attack stealthiness [51], we give a distribution
of recommendation performance differences between the models
before and after the attacks. The distribution includes various victim
models and real-world datasets. To clearly demonstrate the differ-
ences, we use HR@3 to evaluate, as shown in Fig. 5.

From the figure,
we can find that DrunkAgent does not induce drastic changes in
the overall performance, which indicates its unnoticeable.
4.5  Defense Strategies to Attacks
Following the works [49, 51], we use the paraphrasing defensive
strategy (Para.) via OpenAI-GPT-o1 to combat the attacks of agentic
models. As can be seen from Fig. 6, DrunkAgent is still transferable,
which shows that DrunkAgent is robust to such defenses. Moreover,
DrunkAgent still performs better than the baselines after defenses
were introduced, as shown in Fig. 6 and Table 1. Interestingly, para-
phrasing enhances our attack to a certain extent, e.g., DrunkAgent
with Para. is more effective than DrunkAgent without Para. on
Office Products. It could be that the substitution of certain new

5  RELATEDWORKS
In recent years, shilling attacks (aka data poisoning attacks) on
conventional RSs [17, 22, 23, 31, 47, 48] have been fully investigated
to perform resilience analysis of RSs. However, such attacks inter-
fere the training process of models by injecting fake user profiles,
which have become less effective even of limited applicable to the
recent RSs that are empowered by LLMs. The main reasons are: 1)
LLM-powered RSs either leverage LLM’s in-context learning ca-
pabilities or fine-tune the LLMs with very little data to improve
recommendations [56], which is not required to retrain the models;
2) there are textual semantic gaps between such attacks and current
models [28]. Hence, there is an urgent need to investigate a novel
textual attack paradigm mainly tailored for the inference phase of
models [51]. There are only two state-of-the-art LLM4RS attacks in
the literature [28, 51], but they are far from practical.
Zhang et al. propose a novel black-box attack [51], where at-
tackers can significantly boost a target item’s exposure by merely
altering its textual content during the testing. Compared with tradi-
tional shilling attacks, such an item representation attack is notably
stealthy [51], as it does not affect the overall recommendation per-
formance, and it should be more efficient due to it perturbs the item
features directly. However, its transferability is limited, due to the
attack is not optimized on any black-box RSs [17, 45]. Moreover, the
attack focuses on perturbing the titles, making them easier to be dis-
covered. Meanwhile, another black-box attack CheatAgent [28] has
been proposed. However, this work perturbs prompt templates and
user profiles of victim LLM4RSs by harnessing LLM-based agents,
which is impractical. The main reasons are: 1) tampering with
prompt templates may induce the overall performance changes,
making the attack perceptible [51]; and 2) it is difficult to inject
interaction records into the specific positions of user profiles, be-
cause historical interactions are usually introduced into prompts in
chronological order [50]. Furthermore, CheatAgent is an untargeted
attack that undermines the model’s overall performance, which is
beyond the scope of this paper, due to we focus on targeted attacks.
In addition, as far as we know, there is no work investigating on
textual attacks against the current agent-powered RSs.
To tackle the above problems, we propose a practical attack
framework, DrunkAgent, for the agent-based RSs with the aim of
promoting target items. Our attack perturbs items’ descriptions to

In this paper, we propose a novel practical attack framework, Drunk-
Agent, for autonomous agent-powered RSs with powerful LLMs.
DrunkAgent launches attacks by carefully crafting adversarial de-
scriptions of the target items on agentic recommendations. In this
way, the memories of the target agents can be effectively perturbed
for achieving the attack objectives. Comprehensive experiments
on real-world datasets show that DrunkAgent achieves state-of-
the-art transferability and imperceptibility under the black-box
settings. Most importantly, we identify a new safety vulnerability
in the current agent4RSs. Our findings not only demonstrate an
effective attack method, but also provide a lens to examine how
memory mechanisms in agent-based recommenders can be influ-
enced through real-world inputs. Our work can facilitate a deeper
understanding of agent vulnerabilities and guide future efforts in
building more robust and trustworthy recommendation agents.
Since the generative agent4RS is rapidly flourishing in the recom-
mendation community, we hope this work can be considered for
building next-generation robust RSs in the future.

A APPENDIX
A.1  DrunkAgent Algorithm
For reproducibility, the overall optimization procedure of Drunk-
Agent is given in Algorithm 1. For the sake of algorithmic trans-
parency, our code will be made available after this work is published.
A.2  Dataset Statistics
Table 2 illustrates the statistics of these datasets. The datasets vary
in size and sparsity, which is suitable for a comprehensive eval-
uation. Following the work [50], we further sample subsets from
the datasets given the expensive API calls. Specifically, considering
the importance and commonness of data sparsity and cold-start in
the recommendation community, we randomly sample 100 users
with 800 interactions (777 items), 99 users with 693 interactions
(619 items) and 98 users with 588 interactions (483 items) from
the three datasets, respectively, allowing to explore more diverse
and practical interaction scenarios. Moreover, the leave-one-out
strategy [15] is used for evaluation, where the split between the

er Systems  Conference’17, July 2017, Washington, DC, USA
training and test set is 9:1. Scaling DrunkAgent for larger datasets
is left as future work.
A.3  Ablation Studies
We remove important components of each module to conduct abla-
tion studies of DrunkAgent. First, we consider remove the surrogate
module (denoted as S𝑢). However, if we remove it, the setting will
transfer from the black-box to the white-box that is impractical and
beyond the scope of this paper. Moreover, due to gpt-3.5-turbo
used for victim models is closed-source and only can be called via
APIs, we cannot obtain logits to optimize the adversarial triggers
like Llama-3 used in the surrogate model does. Hence, we maintain
the surrogate model to ensure more practical settings. We then
remove the greedy optimization algorithm (denoted as G𝑟 ) in the
generation module to observe how the performance changes.

As
illustrated in Table 3, the attack performance moderately degrades
after removing G𝑟 , which demonstrates the effectiveness of the
optimization algorithm. The lack of dramatic performance degrada-
tion is probably due to the effectiveness of our initialization. Finally,
we mask the strategy module (denoted as S𝑡 ). From the table, we
can find that the attack performance decreases by a large margin.
This may be because memory updates during agent-environment
interactions are somewhat robust to non-tailored attacks (this phe-
nomenon can also be observed in the performance of the attack
baselines). Our reported results are built upon AgentCF, and similar
tendencies can be found on AgentRAG and AgentSEQ.
A.4  Parameter Sensitivity
FromTable 3, we can find that the strategymodule contributes more
than the optimization algorithm on DrunkAgent’s attack perfor-
mance. As such, we increase the maximum rounds of optimization
iterations per step of victim models to evaluate the attack success
rates of the attack strategy, where the default value is 2 as suggested
in the paper [50]. As illustrated in Table 4, the success rates of get-
ting the target agents drunk are maintained at 100% regardless of
the number of iterations. For the other attack parameters, based on
our observations, our DrunkAgent is insensitive to them.
A.5  Victim LLM-powered Agentic Models
LLM-driven agents, known for their superior autonomous interac-
tion and decision-making capabilities, are gradually being consid-
ered as next-generation RSs. Compared with some agent-powered
RSs that focus solely on user-side behaviormodeling using universal
LLMs [16, 37], AgentCF [50] emphasizes the modeling of two-sided
interaction relations between user agents and item agents through
the idea of collaborative filtering (CF) [15]. Both kinds of agents
are equipped with memory modules, maintaining the simulated
preferences and tastes of potential adopters involving their intrin-
sic features and acquired behavioral information via autonomous
interactions and collaborative reflection optimizations at each time
step. As such, the memories of the user agents and item agents
can be accordingly updated, enabling the agents to better fit the
real-world interaction behaviors. To conduct personalized recom-
mendations, AgentCF introduces various well-crafted prompting
strategies on the optimized memories. We name the model accord-
ing to the use of the prompting strategy, with the basic one called

A.6  Baselines
The baselines perturb the descriptions of the target items at various
levels, which are described as below.
• Character-level Perturbations. DeepwordBug [12] and
PuncAttack [11] manipulate texts by introducing typos and
inserting punctuation, respectively.
• Word-level Perturbations. TextFooler [18] and BertAttack
[21], respectively, aim to replace words with synonyms or
contextually similar words.
• Sentence-level Perturbations. TrivialInsertion [51] inserts
text content with 𝑘 number of positive words from a pre-
defined word corpus, where the insertion is conducted at the
end of the text. Here, 𝑘 = 2. ChatGPTAttack [51] increases
the stealthiness of TrivialInsertion by rewriting the text con-
tent via GPTs. GPT-3.5-turbo is used and the maximum
number of words is set to 30 here.
We give some examples of adversarial descriptions of target
items generated by the baselines in Table 6. Following the works

feasible due to the real-world item providers is allowed to modify their
